agent_outputs.raw: The draft slide claim that the model 'can guide adjuvant therapy selection across solid tumors' is not justified by the evidence. There are two distinct problems here. First, the study measures prognostic discrimination: which early-stage breast cancer patients had longer progression-free survival under the treatments they actually received. This does not establish treatment benefit heterogeneity, which asks whether a specific treatment causes better outcomes for a specific patient compared to an alternative. A prognostic model and a predictive biomarker are different things. Second, the evidence base is exclusively early-stage breast cancer at one center. Applying this to 'solid tumors' — a category that includes lung, colorectal, gastric, ovarian, and many other cancers with distinct biology, pathology staining patterns, and treatment protocols — requires independent validation in each setting. The model may be a useful prognostic tool for breast cancer in this institutional context, but the therapy-guidance claim across tumor types is unsupported. VERDICT: critique_wins

The claim under debate conflates two conceptually different problems. A prognostic model predicts outcomes under observed treatment patterns; a therapy-selection model identifies which treatment works better for which patient. Predicting who does worse under historical care does not tell you whether changing the treatment would help them. The retrospective, single-institution design with non-randomized treatment assignment makes this conflation worse, not better. Even if we ignore the prognosis-vs-treatment-benefit distinction, the generalization from early-stage breast cancer to all solid tumors is not supported. Different tumor types require different histopathological features, have different treatment modalities, and may exhibit completely different visual patterns in tissue slides. The claim should be narrowed to prognostic stratification in early-stage breast cancer at similar pathology centers. VERDICT: critique_wins

The supporters' argument — that anything improving prognosis estimation is 'naturally relevant to treatment planning' — is the key logical error here. Improved prognosis estimation helps clinicians understand disease severity, but it does not by itself indicate which treatment to select. Treatment selection requires comparative evidence about differential effects of different therapies. The current study provides neither randomized treatment comparison nor multi-tumor validation. Furthermore, even within breast cancer, the retrospective single-center design with treatment confounding limits what can be concluded. Extending the claim across tumor types amplifies all these limitations. The appropriate evidence for a therapy-guidance claim would be prospective studies with comparative arms across each claimed tumor type and treatment context. VERDICT: critique_wins

synthesis: All three assessors converge on the same two core problems: (1) the prognosis-treatment-benefit conflation — predicting survival under historical care does not establish which therapy is better for which patient; and (2) the cross-tumor generalization without evidence — early-stage breast cancer at one center does not support therapy-guidance claims across solid tumors with distinct biology and workflows. Agent 2 and 3 also highlight the retrospective treatment confounding as a compounding issue. The ETD constraint forces a definitive position: the evidence does not support the draft slide claim.
all_issues_raised: retrospective treatment confounding with non-randomized treatment assignment

empirical_test.condition: Prospective multi-center studies estimating treatment-benefit heterogeneity within each claimed tumor type and therapy context
empirical_test.supports_critique_if: Model predicts prognosis but not differential treatment benefit; fails to transfer to non-breast solid tumor settings
empirical_test.supports_defense_if: Studies confirm treatment-benefit heterogeneity prediction beyond standard covariates across multiple tumor types
empirical_test.ambiguous_if: Model shows prognostic value across centers but lacks evidence for regimen-selection capability
